title: The 11 Best Treasury Management Platforms for Startups
subtitle: A ranked analysis of platforms helping startups manage runway, maximize yield on idle cash, and extend their financial runway securely.
audience: Startup CFOs and founders parking and managing runway

editor.name: Top 11 Editorial
editor.credential: Autonomous AI ranking engine — methodology v1.0 weights public
editor.conflict_disclosure: None. The editor of Top 11 is not a candidate on this list.

independence.statement: Top 11 takes no payment from any provider on this list. Scores are computed from a public weighted rubric; methodology weights were locked before entry research began.

changelog.text: Initial publication. Methodology v1.0 weights Yield & Investment Options (30%), Security & Compliance (25%), Liquidity & Access (20%), Platform & Usability (15%), and Integration & Banking Features (10%).

answer_capsule: The best treasury management platform for startups is Mercury, followed by Rho and Meow, for their combination of high yield, robust security, and integrated, startup-friendly banking features.

criteria.name: Yield & Investment Options
criteria.description: Net yield on cash after fees, diversity of low-risk investment options (T-Bills, MMFs), and clarity of fee structures.

criteria.name: Security & Compliance
criteria.description: Strength of regulatory compliance (FINRA, SEC), insurance coverage (FDIC, SIPC), and use of qualified custodians and security protocols (SOC 2).

criteria.name: Liquidity & Access
criteria.description: Speed and ease of moving funds into and out of treasury accounts, including settlement times (T+0, T+1) and withdrawal limits.

criteria.name: Platform & Usability
criteria.description: Intuitive user interface, quality of reporting and forecasting tools, and ease of onboarding for non-expert founders.

criteria.name: Integration & Banking Features
criteria.description: Integration with accounting software (QuickBooks, NetSuite) and availability of adjacent banking services (checking, cards, bill pay).

guide.q: What is the primary goal of a treasury management platform for a startup?
guide.a: The primary goal is twofold: to protect the startup's primary asset—its cash runway—from institutional and inflation risk, and to generate a meaningful, low-risk yield on that cash to extend the runway.

guide.q: How do these platforms generate yield?
guide.a: They typically provide access to investment vehicles that are otherwise difficult for startups to access directly, such as U.S. Treasury Bills (T-Bills), money market funds (MMFs), and repurchase agreements (repos). They also use sweep networks to spread cash across multiple banks to maximize FDIC insurance.

how_to_choose: Assess your primary need: Are you looking for a simple yield-generating account or a full-suite finance platform with integrated banking, spend management, and treasury?
Verify security and insurance: Ensure the provider uses qualified custodians, offers robust FDIC/SIPC insurance coverage, and is SOC 2 compliant.
Evaluate liquidity terms: Understand how quickly you can access your funds (T+0, T+1, etc.), as runway cash needs to be available for payroll and operations.
Compare net yields: Look beyond the advertised rate to understand the net yield after all management fees (typically measured in basis points, bps).

faqs.q: What is treasury management for a startup?
faqs.a: For a startup, treasury management is the strategic management of its cash reserves (runway) to ensure safety, liquidity, and optimal yield. It involves moving funds beyond a standard checking account into low-risk, interest-bearing instruments to protect against inflation and bank risk while earning a return.

faqs.q: How much yield can a startup realistically earn?
faqs.a: Yields are typically benchmarked against the effective federal funds rate. Startups can often earn yields closely tracking short-term U.S. Treasury Bills, which can be significantly higher than traditional business savings accounts. Net yield will be the gross rate minus the platform's fees (e.g., 10-25 bps).

faqs.q: Is it safe to put my startup's runway in these platforms?
faqs.a: Generally, yes, if you choose a reputable provider. Top platforms mitigate risk by using third-party qualified custodians (like BNY Mellon or Goldman Sachs), offering SIPC insurance on securities up to $500,000, and using FDIC sweep networks to extend insurance coverage into the millions.

faqs.q: What's the difference between FDIC and SIPC insurance?
faqs.a: FDIC (Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation) insurance protects cash deposits held at an insured bank, typically up to $250,000 per depositor, per bank. SIPC (Securities Investor Protection Corporation) insurance protects securities (like stocks, bonds, and MMFs) held at a brokerage firm, up to $500,000, in case the firm fails.

faqs.q: When should a startup start thinking about treasury management?
faqs.a: A startup should consider treasury management as soon as it has raised a seed round or has more than 3-6 months of operating expenses sitting idle in a checking account. The opportunity cost of earning near-zero interest is significant, especially with larger cash balances.

honest_disclosures: Most providers on this list are US-focused and primarily serve US-domiciled startups with US bank accounts.
Advertised yields are variable and directly influenced by prevailing interest rates set by the Federal Reserve. They are not guaranteed.
This ranking prioritizes integrated, all-in-one platforms over pure investment-only tools, reflecting the needs of most startups for operational simplicity.

glossary.definition: A type of mutual fund that invests in high-quality, short-term debt instruments like U.S. Treasury Bills. They are considered a very low-risk cash equivalent.

entries.name: Mercury
entries.best_for: Early-stage startups seeking an all-in-one platform that seamlessly combines day-to-day banking with a simple, high-yield treasury product.
entries.verdict: Mercury ranks first because its Mercury Vault product is the most seamlessly integrated and user-friendly treasury solution for the typical venture-backed startup, combining high FDIC insurance limits and competitive yield within an already best-in-class banking platform.
entries.verdict_short: The best all-in-one banking and treasury solution, offering simplicity, security, and competitive yield for early-stage startups.
entries.praise: The platform's simplicity is its greatest strength; moving funds between operating cash and high-yield treasury accounts takes just a few clicks.
entries.praise_short: Unmatched ease of use and seamless integration.
entries.criticism: While competitive, the yields on its money market funds may sometimes lag behind more specialized, treasury-only platforms.
entries.criticism_short: Yields can be slightly lower than pure-play competitors.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.

entries.name: Rho
entries.best_for: Scaling startups that need a unified finance platform for treasury, corporate cards, and automated accounts payable in a single dashboard.
entries.verdict: Rho earns the second spot by offering a powerful, integrated finance automation platform where treasury is a core component, not an add-on, making it ideal for companies scaling their finance operations beyond basic banking.
entries.verdict_short: A powerful, integrated finance platform for scaling companies, combining treasury, AP automation, and spend management.
entries.praise: Rho's ability to manage T-bills directly alongside corporate spend and bill pay provides a holistic view of a company's financial position.
entries.praise_short: Holistic view of cash and spend.
entries.criticism: The platform can be more complex to navigate for first-time founders compared to simpler solutions like Mercury.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.

entries.name: Meow
entries.best_for: Startups prioritizing maximum yield and a straightforward, dedicated treasury experience without the complexity of a full banking suite.
entries.verdict: Meow secures a top-three position due to its singular focus on providing high, transparent yields on corporate cash through a simple and secure platform, making it the best choice for startups that already have a banking relationship and want a specialized treasury partner.
entries.verdict_short: A simple, secure, and high-yield platform perfect for startups wanting a dedicated treasury solution.
entries.praise: Meow consistently offers some of the most competitive yields in the market by providing direct access to MMFs and T-bills with transparent, low fees.
entries.criticism: As a pure treasury platform, it lacks the integrated banking features like checking accounts, cards, or bill pay found in all-in-one solutions.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.

entries.name: Arc
entries.best_for: Growth-stage startups looking for a holistic capital partner that combines high-yield treasury with venture debt and other financing options.
entries.verdict: Arc is ranked fourth for its unique combination of a robust, high-yield treasury suite with integrated financing solutions, making it a strategic financial partner for startups looking beyond simple cash management.
entries.verdict_short: A strategic platform combining high-yield treasury with integrated startup financing and banking products.
entries.praise: The ability to manage runway and access growth capital from the same platform provides significant strategic advantages.
entries.criticism: The user interface for the treasury product, while functional, is less polished than some top-ranked competitors.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.

entries.name: Brex
entries.best_for: Startups already embedded in the Brex ecosystem for corporate cards and expense management who want to consolidate their finances.
entries.verdict: Brex places fifth by offering a solid treasury product that is a convenient and logical choice for the thousands of startups already using its best-in-class spend management platform, despite its treasury features not being as central as those of higher-ranked rivals.
entries.verdict_short: A convenient treasury option for companies already using Brex for cards and expense management.
entries.praise: The integration with Brex's corporate card and expense software provides a single source of truth for a company's cash-out and cash-in.
entries.criticism: The treasury product can feel like a secondary feature within the broader Brex platform, and accessing detailed investment information is less intuitive.
entries.criticism_short: Treasury feels like a secondary feature.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.

entries.name: Vesto
entries.best_for: Finance teams looking for a simple, secure, and automated platform to invest idle cash with a focus on T-Bills.
entries.verdict: Vesto earns its spot by providing a highly focused and automated solution for investing in T-Bills, appealing to startups that want a 'set it and forget it' approach to earning yield on their excess cash.
entries.verdict_short: A simple, automated platform for startups to earn yield on idle cash via T-Bills.
entries.praise: The platform's automation features for creating and managing T-bill ladders are excellent for optimizing yield and liquidity.
entries.criticism: It offers fewer investment options compared to platforms that also provide access to a wide variety of money market funds.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.

entries.name: Treasure Financial
entries.best_for: CFOs and finance leaders who want to actively manage their treasury strategy with sophisticated tools and a variety of investment options.
entries.verdict: Treasure Financial is a strong contender for its focus on providing more granular control and a wider array of investment choices, making it a good fit for more financially sophisticated startups that want to go beyond simple MMFs.
entries.verdict_short: A sophisticated platform for CFOs who want granular control over their treasury strategy.
entries.praise: Their 'Treasure Reserve' account offers a compelling, liquid, high-yield option backed by government securities.
entries.criticism: The platform is less intuitive for non-finance users, and the onboarding process can be more involved than with simpler competitors.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.

entries.name: Relay
entries.best_for: Small businesses and early-stage startups that need to segregate funds into multiple checking and savings accounts for clear financial management.
entries.verdict: Relay makes the list not as a high-yield treasury platform, but as a superior cash organization tool whose high-interest savings accounts serve as a 'Treasury 1.0' solution, perfect for startups not yet ready for complex instruments.
entries.verdict_short: Excellent for cash organization with multiple accounts, offering a simple first step into treasury management.
entries.praise: The ability to open up to 20 checking accounts and 2 savings accounts makes it incredibly easy to implement profit-first or bucket-based budgeting.
entries.criticism: The interest rates on savings, while good for a bank, do not compete with the yields from T-Bills or MMFs offered by specialized treasury platforms.
entries.criticism_short: Yields are not competitive with MMFs.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.

entries.name: Flint
entries.best_for: Startups wanting a dead-simple, mobile-first experience for earning high yield on their cash with maximum security.
entries.verdict: Flint is a noteworthy new entrant focused on radical simplicity, offering a clean, mobile-friendly interface to access high-yield, government-backed securities, making it a great choice for founders who value ease of use above all.
entries.verdict_short: A new, radically simple platform for startups to access high-yield government securities with ease.
entries.praise: The user experience is exceptionally clean and straightforward, removing nearly all friction from the process of moving and investing cash.
entries.criticism: As a newer platform, it lacks the extensive integration options and long track record of more established players.
entries.criticism_short: Fewer integrations and shorter track record.

risk_signals.summary: As a newer market entrant, the platform has a shorter operational history.

entries.name: Mayfair
entries.best_for: Companies seeking a white-glove, managed approach to treasury with active portfolio management and advisory.
entries.verdict: Mayfair distinguishes itself by offering a more hands-on, managed service, making it a good choice for startups that want to outsource their treasury strategy to experts rather than using a self-serve platform.
entries.verdict_short: A managed treasury service for startups wanting an expert-led, hands-off approach to cash management.
entries.praise: The access to dedicated advisors for building a custom treasury policy is a significant value-add for teams without deep financial expertise.
entries.criticism: This high-touch model can result in higher fees and less direct control compared to fully self-serve platforms.
entries.criticism_short: Higher fees and less direct control.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.

entries.name: Modern Treasury
entries.best_for: Tech-forward, later-stage startups that need to build a sophisticated, multi-bank payment operations and cash management system via API.
entries.verdict: Modern Treasury is our wildcard because it's not a direct investment platform; it's a payment operations infrastructure that *enables* sophisticated treasury. It's for the Series C+ company that wants to programmatically manage cash across multiple bank partners, not the seed-stage startup looking to park cash for yield.
entries.verdict_short: An API-first platform for building custom, multi-bank payment operations and treasury systems, not for direct investing.
entries.praise: It provides unparalleled power and flexibility for automating complex money movements, reconciliation, and managing cash across multiple banking relationships.
entries.criticism: It does not directly offer yield-bearing products; it is a software layer that connects to your existing banks, requiring significant engineering resources to implement.
entries.criticism_short: Doesn't offer yield; requires engineering lift.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-06-03.
